Forests of the Sierra de San Juan Cosalá

The forested mountains above Lake Chapala, the Sierra de San Juan Cosalá, are part of the Bajío Dry Forests ecoregion of west central Mexico. Due to agricultural, residential, and industrial development in the Bajío, only about 5% of the original habitat remains. So the Sierra de San Juan Cosalá is a very significant remnant of original forest that remains largely undeveloped, and is at least partially protected.

The forests here consist almost entirely of angiosperms (flowering, mostly deciduous leaf trees and cactus species). Other than the one ocote (pine) on the mesa, there are no native conifers (gymnosperms). The few other pines that are present are likely introduced.

The mountains have two main forest zones. The lower elevation and south facing slopes are dry subtropical deciduous forest. The higher elevations near the ridge tops and down the north facing slopes, are dominated by oak forests. I have tried to assemble descriptions of the dominant and more visible species of these two zones. The dense understory of smaller trees, shrubs, vines and flowering plants, particularly in the subtropical deciduous zone, are too diverse and beyond the scope of this project.

The subtropical deciduous zone has a great diversity of species. The variety can be quite challenging at first, but by looking closely at the forms, leaves, bark, seeds and flowers, the individual species start to stand out. The overall texture changes dramatically from the exuberant green of the rainy summer season, to the brown dry season when most species lose their foliage.

The oak forest zone, though seemingly simpler, is more complex than meets the eye. Mexico is the world's oak diversity hotspot, hosting around 160 Quercus species, including white and red oak lineages, with endemic varieties adapted to the diverse Mexican ecosystems. Oaks also readily hybridize. So there are many different species of oak here and they can only be distinguished by often subtle differences in leaf shapes and bark textures. I have identified a few of the more prominent oak species, I'm sure there are many more. Also there are a few other species intermixed with the oak, and the understory is largely open, so they are easier to identify.

I found it quite rewarding to start to recognise the different species, and gradually find the perplexing diversity become more familiar. When I started out, I had been dismayed at the scarcity of botanical resources available, so I hope this guide will help and give you an appreciation of the remarkable ecosystem of the Sierra de San Juan Cosalá.

Identification Features and Terminology

In order to identify different species you need to examine the leaves, bark, flowers, seeds and general form. Much of botanical terminology is detailed and not commonly understood, so I have used more general terms where possible. In any case, understanding a few basic terms is necessary.

Leaves are a good place to start, and here is a chart of some terms. You don't need to know most of them, but they show you the variations, and a few are essential.

First note if it is a simple or a compound leaf. A simple leaf has a single, undivided blade attached to a twig by a petiole, whereas a compound leaf's blade is divided into multiple, separate leaflets attached to a central stalk (rachis).

On simple leaves first note the shape. Then look at the margin, are they smooth (entire) or serrated, spiny, or lobed. Then the venation, are the veins pinnate (parallel) or arctuate (bending toward the tip).

There are a lot of trees with compound leaves locally, mainly pinnate and bi-pinnate leaf forms. Here is a pinnate leaf from a Cuachalalate. Note how it has a central stem or rachis, with the leaves arranged in pairs along it.

Next is a bipinnate leaf from a Tepeguaje. It has a central stem with secondary stems in pairs, with the tiny leaflets in pairs on the secondary stems, forming a feathery look.

Bark colors and textures can give you more information. And if flowers are present they can be really distinctive. Also the seeds or pods are strong indications.

I have included three large cactus species and the tree-like Nolina, which are succulents not trees, but commonly present in the forests.

If you combine all this with the general form, size and location, you are well on your way. I have included pictures of these details with all the species, so you can quickly narrow it down.

List of Species

Species of lower elevations:

Tepejuaje - Lysiloma acapulcense

Quebracho - Lysiloma divaricatum

 

Copal - Bursera bipinnata

Copal - Bursera penicillata

Papel - Bursera fagaroides

Papel - Bursera simaruba

 

Rock Fig - Ficus petiolaris

Strangler Fig - Ficus cotinifolia

 

Ceiba - Ceiba aesculifolia

Guaje - Leucaena leucocephala

Mexican pistache - Pistacho silvestre

Cuachalalate - Amphipterygium adstringe

Guanacaste - Enterolobium cyclocarpum

Myrtle - Eugenia capuli

Chilicote - Erythrina flabelliformis

Cazahuate - Ipomoea arborescens

Tree daisy - Montanoa grandiflora

Huizache - Vachellia pennatula

Cojoton - Tabernaemontana donnell

Nopal - Opuntia streptacantha

Cardón - Pachycereus pecten aboriginum

Species of Higher Elevations

Encino Roble - Quercus rugosa

Encino Chicharron - Quercus crassifolia

Encino Magnolia - Quercus magnoliifolia

Mexican Laurel oak - Quercus laurina

 

Mexican Madrona - Arbutus xalapensis

Nolina - Nolina longifolia

Maguey - Agave inaequidens

Note: I am not a professional botanist, and though I have researched these species extensively, there could be errors.
